content.claim: Hungary has one of the OECD's strongest socioeconomic performance gradients, with family economic, social, and cultural status explaining more than 20% of variance in reading, science, and mathematics achievement (compared to 12-16% OECD average), and school-type segregation is a major structural channel for this inequality.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: PISA measures 15-year-olds already sorted into tracks, so cannot by itself isolate the causal share attributable to the 6/8-year gimnázium mechanism specifically versus the general 3-way secondary tracking system

The OECD finding that disadvantaged students in Hungary have less than one-in-eight chance of attending school with high achievers is cross-sectional, not causal evidence of gimnázium selection's effect
World Bank attribution to tracked structure is interpretive rather than based on controlled comparison
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
